speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Date-- January 11, 1996. Survivor is Joseph Berger. Interviewer-- Tiffany Cale. City-- New York. State-- New York. Country-- USA. Language-- English.

speaker: interviewee
text: New York, USA, English, yeah?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. [LAUGHS] OK, January 11, 1996-- the survivor is Joseph Berger. The interviewer is Tiffany Cale. New York, New York, USA, English. Hi.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hi.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me when you were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: September 20, 1937.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what city?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a little town on the Hungarian-Yugoslav border called Subotica.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: S-U-B-O-T-I-C-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And what are your parents' full names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's name was Anna Goldner, G-O-L-D-N-E-R. And of course, when she married my dad, it became Berger. And my dad's name was Imre [? Mirkl ?] Berger.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell your father's name for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. His first name was I-M-R-E, which is a Hungarian name, and [? Mirkl ?] [? M-I-R-K-L ?], which was a Serbian name, and, of course, Berger.

speaker: interviewer
text: How far-- do you know how  far the town that you were born was from the Hungarian border?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was just practically on the border. As a matter of fact, they kept shifting hands. It was Hungarian, it was Yugoslav, it was Hungarian, it was Yugoslav. And it was-- well, the area was part of the Austro-Hungarian empire. And after World War I, they decided that the town was going to be Yugoslav, and later on, it became Hungarian.

speaker: interviewer
text: What language did   the people in [? that ?] town speak? Both. Both. Actually, they spoke Serbian, they spoke Hungarian. There were some folks, actually, that spoke German. But primarily, these people were speaking Hungarian. How many Jews were in the community?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think almost 10,000. So it was quite-- it was quite a large community. About 10% of the population was Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: [? What ?]-- did you know what, most of the Jews that lived in that community, what kind of occupations they--

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything. It ranged from everything. It ranged from farmers and [? like ?] shopkeepers and physicians and pharmacists and teachers-- the whole-- the whole range, the whole gamut of society. There were Orthodox Jews, and there were non-Orthodox Jews. And there were the very, very, very strongly Orthodox Jews. Some were associated with the Satmar-- S-A-T-M-A-R-- sect. And then there were the  others. One was called the Neolog, which were-- they were more reformed. And, of course, then with the practicing Jews, who were practicing Jews, in that they had Jewish homes and observed all the holidays, but they did not necessarily keep a kosher home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell the difference between these Jews by their outward appearance?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, of course, those who were from the Satmar, they dress like they still do in Williamsburg today-- Williamsburg, New York, not Williamsburg, Virginia. And the others were very, very assimilated. I mean, the only way you could really tell is, on a very hot day, perhaps, when nobody else would be wearing a hat, they'd be wearing a hat-- the men, that is. The women really did not wear Sheitels, except for the very orthodox and very religious. Although, in my family, some wore Sheitels, and others did not. But primarily, the people that-- in our circle-- not necessarily our family, but our circle were mainly practicing Jews who knew they would were Jewish, who were Zionists and non-kosher.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did Jews live with people that weren't Jewish? Or did they live in more isolated-- among other Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I have to tell you that my time in Subotica was limited. I-- that's not-- that's where I was born. My father was a physician. He was a pediatrician. And he had practiced in Subotica, and we also lived in Belgrade, Yugoslavia. And until the war, primarily Belgrade is where we lived. When we came back, the war had already started, and obviously some things a little bit different then, because then the war was on. But I would say that Jews and non-Jews lived together. There was no such thing as a ghetto, if that's the question. I mean, just the same way that there are Jewish neighborhoods and Chinese neighborhoods and black neighborhoods and Hispanic neighborhoods, some by social need, some by desire, but there was no specific ghetto, if that's what you're alluding to.

speaker: interviewer
text: So [INAUDIBLE] in your estimates, some Jews would live with-- with people that weren't Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course. Right. I mean, the Hungarian Jews and-- and, you know-- even though you really didn't know what it was, it was a legacy of the Austria-Hungarian empire. And the people really, really were assimilated.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. So people-- people that were Jewish were friends with people that weren't Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you would-- if you were a professional, if you were a doctor, you would treat patients that weren't Jewish, necessarily? Would people primarily go to Jewish, like, physicians? Like how integrated was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's-- I-- I would say that it was as integrated-- it was one of most integrated societies that existed, as far as Jews were integrating into the mainstream. So that, really, there was not any clear cut-- you went to Dr. Berger. You didn't go to the Jewish Dr. Berger or the Christian Dr. So-and-so or the Muslim Dr. So-and-so. You went to Dr. Berger, because Dr. Berger was the pediatrician, or-- or Mr. So-and-so was the attorney, or Mr. So-and-so had the butcher shop, or what have you. So it really wasn't-- obviously, a kosher butcher shop is a different situation. But otherwise, there was really, really-- I mean, as a kid, I really didn't know the difference.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you live there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, off and on between Subotica and Belgrade until the summer of 1944, so from September of 1937 to the summer of 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: I didn't understand. Did you move to Belgrade? Or you--

speaker: interviewee
text: We moved to Belgrade until the war started. When the Germans came into Belgrade in 1941, we fled. And we went back to Subotica. Now, Subotica then had already become a present that the Germans, for alliance with the Hungarians, gave to the Hungarians. And it became-- it was called Szabadka, which was a Hungarian way of pronouncing the name of town. This changed several of the vowels, and it became Szabadka instead of Subotica. And that's the part of it I remember, when we came back. Obviously, when we came back-- I was 3 and 1/2, so I didn't remember it before we went away, but when we came back, I remember it when we had to come back from Belgrade.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were 3 and a 1/2 when you came back from Belgrade?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So how many-- do you know how many  years you lived in Belgrade?

speaker: interviewee
text: 3 And 1/2 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, we  may come for weekends to Subotica, you know, but our primary-- primary residence was in Belgrade, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Do you have any other children in your  family?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have a sister-- an old sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was she born?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was born February 14, 1935. So she was 2 and 1/2 years older than me.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And how-- what is her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Myriam.

speaker: interviewer
text: Myriam. Can you spell her name for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: M-Y-R-I-A-M.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her-- her current last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her current last name is Danber, D-A-N-B-E-R. And she's a retired school teacher.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any recollections of your family life, either-- any-- any time before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I do. Well, let's-- I'd like to go over-- well, before the war, my whole-- my-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] my family life primarily was in Belgrade, except for the high holidays, when we would come to Subotica to be with the entire family. My father's family was not a wealthy family, but his father was a shamas in the synagogue. And he was a very, very hard working man. And he was able to send, on his meager income, two sons to the university, which was a very, very big thing at the time. He sent my Uncle Joseph, after whom I am named, to Berlin, where he was a-- a chemical engineer. And he was in a laboratory that were working on the development of plastics. They were working with Bakelite. And unfortunately, the blast furnace oven blew up and crushed him and killed him. My dad was studying medicine in Berlin at the time. And he got married, and when he had his first male child, his only male child, I was named after my Uncle Joseph. That's how come my name is Joseph. My mother's family came from what was very well to do, who became very well to do. They had a factory called [NON-ENGLISH], means Brothers Goldner-- three brothers who had the largest, if not the only, factory that had-- that produced sinks, bathtubs, ice boxes, refrigerators for all of that area of Yugoslavia and Hungary. The factory itself had its own rail siding. There was a 15-bed infirmary for the workers. My grandfather was a very, very progressive man as far as that's concerned. And the-- the family lived in the same place where the factory was. There was a main entrance with an archway from the street, and the two brothers lived on either side of the archway. The various families lived there. And it was connected in the middle. And I recall that very-- very clearly because that was a huge hallway, and we'd have maybe 40 or 50 or 60 people on high holidays for dinners. And towards the back was the factory. And then, of course, the rail siding, et cetera, et cetera, what have you. [BACKGROUND NOISE] In Belgrade, when we lived in Belgrade, we lived in a house. It was a corner house. My father was a very successful pediatrician. I know we lived-- my dad had his office, similar to where I have mine now, on the ground floor of the building where we lived. And we lived several floors up. Whether it was the third or fourth or fifth floor, I don't recall. But we were well off. I had my room. My sister had my-- her own room. We had a chauffeur. We had a cook. We had a maid. And we lived rather well. And everything was just hunky-dory until the Germans came in. My dad had been mobilized. And he was in the Army, Yugoslav Army, when the Germans came in. And they came early. And we ran down to my father's office, where-- there was a room where he had been using the X-ray machines, and it was lead-lined, so we thought we would be a little safer, just in case the house took a direct bombing hit. Of course, it was not my idea. I was a little kid. Right after the bombing was over, we hopped into our car. We had a small English car. I think was an Austin. I really don't know-- small car. And one of the things that-- that-- that stays in my mind to this day, we had taken flour and sugar, and we were driving, and we were trying to get everybody who had any possibility to get to Hungary, was trying to get to Hungary. Because Hungary people are rather safe, and if you spoke Hungarian, things were OK-- or we thought so, anyway. And my mother's entire family-- my father had a very small family. My father had just his mother. His father had died, his brother had died, and he had his mother and some perhaps distant relatives in this country, which we were never able to track down. And so we left. And I remember the Germans came back. And we-- coming out, we have to run out of the car and lay in a ditch until the bombing was over-- bombing and strafing. They really came with the Stukas, those dive bombers. They really were treetop level and just merciless. They're having a field day, just shooting and strafing. And our car took a hit. And it's like you see in the movies, where-- whether it was flour or a large sack of sugar, but it hit the trunk of the car. And you could see the-- the sugar trail coming out of the-- whenever I see one of those films in the movie theaters, I always think back of my youth. It took us a fairly arduous trip. And I really don't recall it. We had to cross rivers. And we had to-- my mother buried her jewelry someplace. We took a raft across. Finally, we got to the part of Yugoslavia that had been returned to Hungary, where my mother's family was. And we stayed in Subotica, which now had become Szabadka. Now, my language at that time was Serbian. And, of course, my mother spoke perfect Hungarian because she was from Hungary. My dad wasn't there. The rest of the family had lived in [INAUDIBLE], spoke Hungarian. So I was-- [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewer
text: Your father wasn't with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was in the Army.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right? And he was-- he was in the Army. I mean, the war was on. Germany had invaded Yugoslavia. Yugoslavia had decided they were going to resist Germany. And so he was in the Army. He was an officer in the Army, and he was not with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were a lot of Jews drafted into the Army?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Jews were not thought as anything different than anybody else in Yugoslavia at that time-- the Yugoslavs, the Serbs. The Croats-- that's a different story. But the Serbs never thought of the Jews as being the enemy, or they were never, ever very partisan towards the Germans or their way of thinking.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we arrived to members of my mother's family, and finally we worked our way back to Subotica. And then I was not allowed to go out of the house. We stayed with some relatives. And I was allowed to go in the courtyard, which was an enclosed courtyard.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was on like a farm. There was a smokehouse where they would smoke the meat. And they would-- I remember a fun time pickling fruits and jams and what have you. But I was not allowed to go out until I forgot all the Serbian that I knew. And I could speak Hungarian. And I had to speak it fluently, so in case somebody stopped me on the street, I wouldn't be some-- I wouldn't be an 'enemy,' quote unquote. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My sister started attending school. She attended the Jewish school. And the Jewish school was on the grounds of the great synagogue. It was a huge, huge synagogue in Subotica, which is still there today. And there were Estee Lauder's son and other people collecting money for the restoration and renovation-- not only the great synagogue in Budapest, but also in Subotica. And it was the smallest synagogue for the winter. There was a large synagogue for the high holidays and summer, and then there was a smaller synagogue for the winter time. And the same grounds, which was probably a huge, huge square block, there was an area which was the elementary school. And my sister attended an elementary school there, and sometimes we'd come pick her up. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I was born-- So your sister spoke Hungarian?

speaker: interviewee
text: Also, yes, of course. She had to learn how to speak Hungarian as well.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long do you think that you weren't allowed out?

speaker: interviewee
text: About three months. I-- I happen to be gifted with languages. And when you're such a young kid, you know, your vocabulary is very limited to start with. And so my mother and my father had spoken Hungarian at home at times when they didn't want me to understand. So after a while, you start understanding things. I'd say three months, two or three months, not-- maximum.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your father's mother with you? Was your family living with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's mother was there. She was not-- The only people who were in Belgrade, I think, was my mother's younger brother, my mother, my father, my sister, and I. And my father, of course, went in the Army at the time. So there was just the-- there was just the four of us from our immediate family. The rest of our entire family lived in Subotica, was in the town or in the neighborhood in the area.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you went into what-- Subotica, which was now part Hungary, did you cross a border? Are your escaping?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had to cross a border, of course, from Yugoslavia. And that's where we went across the river on rafts. And I don't know exactly when, where and exactly how, but that's-- that's how we did it. And, we had to, of course, after the car got shot up, we had to abandon our car. We didn't have a car any longer.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, where there-- was anybody allowed to cross over?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you had to sneak across the border. I mean, there was-- the war was fluid. It had just started. It was really just starting then at that-- in that part of the world. And the crossing over was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] illegal. And, you know, there were always people-- there are no borders that cannot be crossed. You have to have God, you have to have money, you have to have pull, you have to luck. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So my-- my-- primarily, my memories were very, very fond. They're very nice memories, living in Subotica, you know, during the first part of the war. And-- we lived there, I think, perhaps, till 1943-- for two years, 1943.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very nice. I didn't have to go to school. I was a young kid. I was-- since I was born in September-- anybody that was born after September 1st didn't go to school. You have to be-- you have to be six by September 1. So even later on when we lived in Budapest, I did not have to go to school because I was born September 20th, and I had to wait for the following year, which would have been September of '44. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: So we did everything. I mean, we-- we enjoyed a very nice life. We lived with my grandmother. After I learned how to speak Hungarian, we lived with my-- with my grandma at her house-- my mother's mother, the Goldner house.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my other grandmother, my father's mother, lived in town as well. And we visited with her, and we attended all the holidays, went to synagogue and played. We used to go to cemetery on Sundays sometimes and did everything as if-- to me, I didn't-- I was not aware that there was a war going on or any problems going on. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] One day, my parents-- then, things changed. They sent a German officer with his Shepherd to stay with us-- not with us, but to stay-- you know, you had to house them. We had to house them in my grandmother's house. They-- he was billeted there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he was billeted there. He had to have been a nice person. I mean, he was not mean or nasty to us. You know, he came, and he went and never bothered us. You remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah, absolutely.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember being afraid?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I was not afraid, because you know, he had a nice, nice, nice dog. He kept to his room. All I know is that my sister and I had to share a room instead of each room. We just took one of the rooms. And again-- nothing. Then one day, they took my mother and father away. And when my father came back, he had all-- most of all his teeth missing. He was taken away by the Gestapo. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in-- end of '43 or maybe beginning of '44. I don't remember exactly. And after that-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we moved to Budapest.

speaker: interviewer
text: During the time that you were living there, did your father come back to visit? Or--

speaker: interviewee
text: My father-- oh, I'm sorry. I never told you, my father escaped. [INTERPOSING VOICES] He was a prisoner-- he was a prisoner. And-- he was a-- he was a prisoner. I mean--

speaker: interviewer
text: So he got captured?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yugoslav surrendered. It's not that they captured-- I mean, they surrendered. The Germans defeated the Yugoslavians, they did everybody else in the early years of the war. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he-- he was a physician. He was an officer, but he was a physician in the Army. And he overpowered-- he and some people overpowered some guards. And they took their uniforms, Hungarian uniforms. Now, my father spoke Hungarian perfectly without an accent. So they commandeered a truck. And he hopped into a truck, and he drove and drove and drove. The only problem was that he-- I remember he told me that he could not find shoes to fit him. So he had this Hungarian army uniform on with not-Hungarian-Army-issue shoes. And they were sitting in the truck for three or four days, afraid to get out of the truck because somebody might see their shoes. So-- and then he finally found-- he knew-- he knew where we-- the only place we would go if we were still alive. And we would not have stayed in Belgrade. So he came, and he was with us in Subotica, and he was there. And then we made trips between Subotica and Budapest. We went to live in Budapest. My father was not working any longer. When we lived in Budapest, I don't believe my father was working. He did not have an office, as he would. We lived there, and things were not too bad. We lived, as I best recall, on the street not far from the Danube. I mean, it was Budapest on either side of the Danube. We lived in Pest, not Buda. Buda is on one side, and Pest-- so we lived in Pest. And my aunt and uncle, my mother's older brother and his wife, also lived in town. And I-- my father's mother, we got her a place to stay. I remember I had the chicken pox when I was in Budapest. And I was there until we went to concentration camp. Now, interestingly enough and strangely, things that I remember-- I was watching that little film that Spielberg had put on last week on TBS. And in the newspapers, there's a photograph of two little brothers, maybe two years old, two and a half years old, each with a Star of David on their heart. Interestingly enough, you had to be six. If you were under six, you didn't have to wear a star. And I was so upset and so annoyed and so disappointed that I didn't have to wear a star. I wanted  to wear a star just like-- just everybody else in the family. And I-- you know, I didn't-- my parents had a difficult time explaining to me that I really didn't need it,  why I didn't need to wear it, and that it was a good thing that I didn't need to wear it. Because I didn't have the so-called Semitic appearance-- I was little kid, I didn't have the star, I spoke Hungarian perfectly, I ran in the street, I played ball with all the kids. And as I said, there was no ghetto. So the kids that I played with were-- could have been young, Jewish children, or they could have been Gentile children for all I know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you move to Budapest because the situation wasn't safe?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, we had to move to Budapest-- right. We had to move to Budapest. We-- my father wasn't working. My mother wasn't working. And we had to move-- we moved to Budapest, and had to move to Budapest. Until the-- near the very end, Hungarian Jews were relatively safe. I mean, if you look at the history, the Hungarian Jews survived practically the entire war, until near the end of 1944. After-- I mean, most of the Hungarians Jews were alive on D-Day. It was only after that that they started getting Hungarian Jews, when that raid destroyed the entire Jewry of all of Europe where they were, but the Hungarian Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: So anybody who was from the former Austro-Hungarian Empire, whether you were Czech, whether you were Yugoslav, whether you were Romanian, if you could speak Hungarian, well, of course, you went to Hungary because you were relatively safe in Hungary-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] early on. Later on, things got pretty bad. I mean, things got so bad that the-- the SS had to protect the Jews from the fascists. That's how bad things finally got in Hungary.

speaker: interviewee
text: But-- so we lived in Budapest. And then first came the laws that-- they-- they were not taking people yet, at that time, to concentration camp. But they were taking men to forced labor. And my mother  had three brothers. The oldest one died someplace near Russia in a forced labor camp. My--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know approximately when?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't. No. My-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] mother's middle brother, younger brother, and my father were taken to a place call Garagn. And don't ask me how to spell it. I think it's G-A-R-A-G-N. And they were there in the-- for a short period of time. My father being a physician, they needed physicians, so they let him come back. And we were together in Budapest.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how-- you knew when you were in Budapest, that you probably had some awareness that something was going on.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, absolutely. Absolutely. Absolutely.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you saw the adoption of laws.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the law-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] You know, the-- the Aryan laws were put into effect. There was no walled ghetto. But it's my perception that Jews lived-- had to live in a certain area. However, there were no walls and no dogs and guards-- I mean, nothing like that had in Eastern Europe. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] of course, jobs were scarce. However, Hungarian Jews are, you know, sort  of a strange mix of either assimilated or [NON-ENGLISH], the very, very, very, very, you know, orthodox. And as a matter of fact, the-- the assimilated Jews used to call the very orthodox or Hasidic Jews 'Fins'-- Fin Poland, Fin Lithuania, Fin Latvia, [NON-ENGLISH], meaning 'from Poland,' 'from Lithuania,' 'from Latvia,' 'not from here,' You know, it was-- it was an expression. I mean, the chief of police, once upon a time, Budapest, was a-- was a Jew and in a very, very high position. So the Jews were very, very assimilated. And when the fascists turned on them, it was such a shock that-- and in such an assimilated place. I mean, the Admiral Horthy through marriage had Jewish relatives. It was-- it was-- it was-- it was amazing. But things got bad. Now, the reason I'm sitting here today, and that I am not someplace in Eastern Europe, [INAUDIBLE] Eastern Europe, is because I happened to have been living in Budapest, and my father happened to have been a Zionist. And thanks to a man called Kastner-- Rezso-- R-E-Z-S-O, with a two-- umlaut-- Kastner, K-A-S-T-N-E-R-- he was able to save a significant number of Hungarian Jews. And probably that's why-- I was so young, and I-- I survived the war, even though it was a concentration camp.
